description.summary: Psychometry is the alleged paranormal ability to obtain information about an object's history — its past owners, locations, or events surrounding it — by physical contact. The term was coined by American physician Joseph Rodes Buchanan in 1842.
description.full: Psychometry (also called token-object reading or object reading) is a claimed psi modality in which a sensitive holds or touches an object and reports impressions of its history, owners, or associated events. American physician and Eclectic Medical Institute professor Joseph Rodes Buchanan coined the term in 1842, theorising that all things give off persistent emanations that could be 'measured' by trained sensitives; he developed the concept further in his Manual of Psychometry (1893). Geologist William Denton extended the practice into 'geological psychometry' with The Soul of Things (1863), in which his sister and wife reported impressions from rock and fossil specimens. Through the late 19th and early 20th centuries psychometry was a popular feature of Spiritualist seances and stage performances. Selected mediums — notably Stefan Ossowiecki in interwar Poland and Gerard Croiset in the mid-20th-century Netherlands — were tested by parapsychologists and remain reference cases in the field. Modern parapsychology absorbs psychometry-like tasks into broader ESP / clairvoyance protocols. The mainstream scientific consensus is that there is no replicable evidence for psychometry; apparent successes are well explained by cold reading, confirmation bias, and selective reporting. Police agencies overwhelmingly do not consider psychic tipsters useful.

etymology.origin: Coined in 1842 by Joseph Rodes Buchanan from Greek psychē ('soul, mind') and metron ('measure')
etymology.meaning: Literally 'soul-measuring'; the measurement of an object's psychic 'emanations'
etymology.first_use: Buchanan, J. R. (c.1842); developed in his Manual of Psychometry (1893)

related_phenomena.name: Clairvoyance
related_phenomena.description: Direct perception of distant or hidden contemporaneous events

related_phenomena.name: Retrocognition
related_phenomena.description: Knowledge of past events by anomalous means; psychometry is its object-mediated form

related_phenomena.name: Remote viewing
related_phenomena.description: Free-response psi protocol; sometimes uses associated 'targets' analogous to tokens

related_phenomena.name: Mediumship
related_phenomena.description: Spiritualist practice in which psychometry was a common feature

related_phenomena.name: Psychic detective work
related_phenomena.description: Application of psychometry-like claims to criminal investigations

physical.morphology: Psychometry is an alleged perceptual ability rather than a physical entity; its phenomenology is the practitioner's subjective imagery and impressions arising on contact with an object.

abilities.description: Producing impressions of an object's past history, prior owners, or events surrounding it through physical contact
Producing descriptions of present-day owners or places associated with the object
William Denton's claimed application to rocks and fossils, producing visions of geological epochs
Application to criminal investigations: locating bodies or describing crimes from clothing or possessions

patterns: Practiced by self-described sensitives, often in altered or focused states
Common feature of 19th- and 20th-century Spiritualist seances
Subject to vigorous methodological criticism; explanations include cold reading and selection bias
Modern parapsychology folds psychometry-like tasks into general ESP testing

range.description: Practised principally in 19th- and 20th-century Anglosphere Spiritualism and 20th-century European parapsychology, though similar 'reading-from-objects' practices appear in many divinatory traditions worldwide.

hotspots.name: Eclectic Medical Institute, Cincinnati

location.description: Cincinnati, Ohio, where Buchanan held the chair of physiology

hotspots.notes: Buchanan's institutional base while developing psychometry.

hotspots.name: Utrecht University parapsychology laboratory (Tenhaeff)
hotspots.notes: W. H. C. Tenhaeff's testing of Gerard Croiset, including object-reading protocols.

first_recorded.source: Buchanan, J. R., Journal of Man and Manual of Psychometry (1893)

peak_activity_periods.description: Founding era of psychometry within American Spiritualism (Buchanan, Denton)
Heyday of Spiritualist mediumship featuring psychometric readings
Parapsychological testing of Ossowiecki, Croiset, and other mediums

origins.cultural_roots: Embedded in 19th-century American Spiritualism, mesmerism, and the broader 'reform' science culture of the Eclectic Medical movement.
origins.earliest_accounts: Buchanan's first systematic descriptions appeared in his Journal of Man (1849–1856) and were consolidated in Manual of Psychometry (1893).
origins.folklore_connections: Resonates with older traditions of relic-veneration, sortilege from personal items, and folk practices of 'reading' objects.

timeline.event: Joseph Rodes Buchanan coins the term 'psychometry' and begins systematic experiments
timeline.significance: Founding moment of the practice as a self-conscious paranormal claim
timeline.source: Buchanan, J. R., Journal of Man (1849–1856); Manual of Psychometry (1893)

timeline.event: Buchanan begins publishing Buchanan's Journal of Man
timeline.significance: Early American Spiritualist / reform venue for psychometry research

timeline.event: William Denton publishes The Soul of Things, extending psychometry to rocks and fossils
timeline.source: Denton, W., & Denton, E. M. F. (1863), The Soul of Things

timeline.event: Buchanan publishes Manual of Psychometry: the Dawn of a New Civilization
timeline.significance: Defining systematic statement of the doctrine

timeline.event: Death of Joseph Rodes Buchanan in San Jose, California
timeline.significance: End of the founder's life; his program continued through Spiritualist popularisers

timeline.event: Stefan Ossowiecki tested by Polish and international parapsychologists, including Charles Richet
W. H. C. Tenhaeff begins systematic testing of Gerard Croiset at Utrecht's parapsychology laboratory
timeline.significance: Mid-20th-century academic testing of object-reading claims

timeline.event: Croiset's death and Tenhaeff scandal: posthumous re-analysis exposes selective reporting
timeline.significance: Major sceptical correction to a well-known psychometry case

history.modern_era: Psychometry survives chiefly in Spiritualist mediumship, in popular psychic-detective literature, and in occasional parapsychology experiments folded into broader ESP / clairvoyance protocols. The mainstream consensus is that there is no replicable evidence for the practice; police departments overwhelmingly report that psychic tips have not assisted investigations, and known 'successful' cases tend to dissolve under careful scrutiny.

notable.name: Buchanan's early letter-reading experiments
notable.description: Buchanan reported experiments in which subjects holding sealed letters described the writers' personalities and circumstances; he interpreted these as evidence of measurable psychic emanations from physical objects.

credibility.notes: Foundational claim of psychometry; would not satisfy modern methodological standards.

sources: Buchanan, J. R., Manual of Psychometry (1893)

notable.name: Denton 'Soul of Things' geological psychometry

location.description: United States; experiments in Boston-area scientific circles

notable.description: Geologist William Denton presented his sister and wife with rock and fossil specimens whose origins he had not disclosed; their reported impressions of geological epochs and origins were collected in The Soul of Things (1863).

credibility.notes: Influential Spiritualist case; not regarded as evidence by modern standards.

sources: Denton, W., & Denton, E. M. F. (1863), The Soul of Things

notable.name: Tenhaeff testing of Gerard Croiset (Utrecht)
notable.description: Dutch parapsychologist W. H. C. Tenhaeff conducted decades of object-reading and 'chair test' experiments with the medium Gerard Croiset. After Croiset's death in 1980, Piet Hein Hoebens's re-analysis showed extensive selective reporting and methodological flaws.

credibility.notes: Often cited in psi literature; widely regarded as a cautionary tale.

sources: Hoebens, P. H. (1981), 'Croiset and Professor Tenhaeff: Discrepancies in Claims of Clairvoyance', Skeptical Inquirer

documentary.name: Manual of Psychometry (Buchanan)
documentary.description: Buchanan's systematic statement of psychometric doctrine, including reports of letter- and object-reading experiments.
documentary.analysis: Foundational text of the field; methodologically inadequate by modern standards.
documentary.source: Buchanan, J. R. (1893), Manual of Psychometry: the Dawn of a New Civilization

documentary.name: The Soul of Things (Denton)
documentary.description: William Denton's account of psychometric impressions taken from rocks and fossils.
documentary.analysis: Influential Spiritualist work; provenance not adequately blinded.
documentary.source: Denton, W., & Denton, E. M. F. (1863)

documentary.name: Hoebens analysis of Croiset / Tenhaeff
documentary.description: Piet Hein Hoebens's investigation of Tenhaeff's published accounts of Gerard Croiset's psychometric and chair-test work.
documentary.analysis: Demonstrated extensive selective reporting; major sceptical correction in mid-20th-century parapsychology.

paranormal.name: Persistent emanations from objects
paranormal.description: Buchanan's original theory: every object retains psychic 'emanations' of its history that a trained sensitive can detect on contact.

paranormal.name: Object-mediated clairvoyance / retrocognition
paranormal.description: Modern parapsychological framing in which the object serves as a focusing 'target' for general clairvoyant or retrocognitive ability rather than as a literal repository of emanations.

skeptical.name: Cold reading and confirmation bias
skeptical.description: Apparent psychometric successes are produced by cold reading (broadly applicable statements, fishing, and observation of cues), confirmation bias, and selective recall of hits.
evidence_cited: Cross-cultural prevalence of comparable performance practices

skeptical.name: Selective reporting and uncontrolled provenance
skeptical.description: Famous psychometry cases (Croiset / Tenhaeff, various Spiritualist mediums) involved selective reporting and inadequate blinding of object provenance.

skeptical.name: No replicable laboratory evidence
skeptical.description: When tested under modern double-blind protocols, psychometry-like tasks produce results indistinguishable from chance.
evidence_cited: Failure of psychic-detective claims to perform above chance

traditions: Stage and platform mediumship featuring object-reading
folklore.beliefs: Psychometry remains a popular feature of contemporary mediumship and is widely depicted in fiction, despite the absence of replicable scientific evidence.

literature: Manual of Psychometry — Joseph Rodes Buchanan (1893)
The Soul of Things — William and Elizabeth Denton (1863)
Various sceptical writings in the Skeptical Inquirer (Hoebens, Nickell, Hyman)
film_tv: The Dead Zone (1983) and TV series (2002–2007)
Numerous depictions of 'psychic detectives' in police procedurals
other: Recurring trope in detective fiction and television

researchers.name: William Denton
researchers.contribution: The Soul of Things (1863); geological psychometry

researchers.name: W. H. C. Tenhaeff
researchers.contribution: Decades of testing of Gerard Croiset; later shown to have selectively reported

organizations.name: Society for Psychical Research
organizations.focus: Has published on psychometry and related claims since 1882

research.ongoing_studies: No active mainstream-scientific research programme specifically on psychometry; occasional inclusion in broader ESP / clairvoyance protocols and in sceptical investigations of psychic-detective claims.

sources.title: Manual of Psychometry: the Dawn of a New Civilization
The Soul of Things; or, Psychometric Researches and Discoveries
Croiset and Professor Tenhaeff: Discrepancies in Claims of Clairvoyance
Cold Reading: How to Convince Strangers That You Know All About Them
